title: HTML5 Canvas element renders centered on the page
description:
Create a React client component at components/ArcadeCabinet.tsx that renders an HTML5 Canvas element. This component must use the "use client" directive since it accesses browser APIs. Use useRef to get a reference to the canvas DOM element.

The canvas should have a native resolution of 256x240 pixels (matching the NES resolution) but be scaled up to fill a reasonable portion of the viewport using CSS (e.g., max-height: 80vh with aspect-ratio preserved via the CSS aspect-ratio property or by calculating width from height). The canvas should be centered both horizontally and vertically on the page using flexbox on the parent container.

The background of the canvas should be filled with a dark blue color (#1a1a2e) using the 2D rendering context in a useEffect. Import and render this component from app/page.tsx. The page itself should have a black (#000000) background. The canvas element should have an id of "game-canvas" for easy reference.
acceptanceCriteria:
- components/ArcadeCabinet.tsx exists with "use client" directive
- A canvas element with id "game-canvas" renders on the page
- Canvas has native width=256 and height=240 attributes
- Canvas is scaled up via CSS while preserving 256:240 aspect ratio
- Canvas is centered horizontally and vertically in the viewport
- Canvas background is filled with dark blue (#1a1a2e)
- Page background is black (#000000)
- useRef is used to access the canvas DOM element
- No console errors when the component mounts

title: Game loop runs at consistent frame rate via requestAnimationFrame
description:
Create the core game loop in lib/game/engine.ts. The engine should export a GameEngine class that takes a canvas element reference in its constructor and stores the 2D rendering context.

Implement a game loop using requestAnimationFrame that runs update(deltaTime) and render(ctx) methods each frame. Calculate deltaTime as the time elapsed since the last frame in seconds. Cap deltaTime at 1/30 (about 0.033s) to prevent physics explosion when the tab loses focus and regains it after a long pause.

Provide start() and stop() methods. start() begins the requestAnimationFrame loop and records the initial timestamp. stop() calls cancelAnimationFrame to halt the loop. Store the animation frame ID so it can be cancelled.

The ArcadeCabinet component should instantiate the GameEngine in a useEffect hook, call start(), and return a cleanup function that calls stop(). For now, the render method should clear the canvas with clearRect and fill it with the dark blue background color (#1a1a2e). The update method can be empty.
acceptanceCriteria:
- lib/game/engine.ts exports a GameEngine class
- Constructor accepts a canvas element and stores the 2D context
- Game loop uses requestAnimationFrame, not setInterval
- deltaTime is calculated in seconds between frames
- deltaTime is capped at 1/30 second maximum
- start() begins the loop, stop() cancels it via cancelAnimationFrame
- Canvas is cleared and filled with background color each frame
- ArcadeCabinet.tsx creates GameEngine in useEffect with cleanup on unmount
- No memory leaks when component unmounts and remounts

title: Game state machine manages attract, playing, paused, game-over, and high-score states
description:
Add a state machine to the GameEngine in lib/game/engine.ts (or create a separate lib/game/state.ts and import it). Define a TypeScript enum or string union type GameState with values: ATTRACT, PLAYING, PAUSED, GAME_OVER, HIGH_SCORE_ENTRY.

The GameEngine should have a currentState property initialized to ATTRACT. Add a setState(newState: GameState) method that updates currentState. The update() and render() methods should use a switch statement on currentState to dispatch to state-specific logic.

For now, each state should render a visually distinct screen so you can verify transitions work:
- ATTRACT: dark blue background with white text "ATTRACT MODE" centered
- PLAYING: dark green background with white text "PLAYING"
- PAUSED: gray background with white text "PAUSED"
- GAME_OVER: dark red background with white text "GAME OVER"
- HIGH_SCORE_ENTRY: dark purple background with white text "HIGH SCORES"

Use ctx.fillText to render the state labels. Set font to a monospace font at 16px. This state machine is the backbone that all future gameplay features plug into.
acceptanceCriteria:
- GameState type exists with ATTRACT, PLAYING, PAUSED, GAME_OVER, HIGH_SCORE_ENTRY values
- GameEngine has a currentState property defaulting to ATTRACT
- setState method transitions between states
- update() dispatches to state-specific update logic via switch
- render() dispatches to state-specific render logic via switch
- Each state renders a different background color and label text
- Text is centered on the canvas using measureText for horizontal centering
- State transitions do not cause errors or memory leaks
- Game always starts in ATTRACT state on page load

title: Keyboard input handler captures arrow keys, WASD, space, and escape
description:
Create an input handling module at lib/game/input.ts. Export an InputManager class that tracks keyboard state.

In the constructor (or a setup() method), add keydown and keyup event listeners on the document. Maintain two data structures: a Set or Map called keysDown that tracks which keys are currently held, and a Set called keysJustPressed that tracks which keys transitioned from up to down this frame (rising edge detection).

Track these keys: ArrowLeft, ArrowRight, ArrowUp, ArrowDown, a, w, s, d, ' ' (space), Escape, Enter, p. On keydown, add the key to keysDown and keysJustPressed. On keyup, remove from keysDown.

Provide these methods:
- isKeyDown(key: string): boolean — returns true while a key is held
- wasKeyPressed(key: string): boolean — returns true only on the first frame a key goes down
- resetFrameState(): void — clears keysJustPressed; call this at the end of each game loop frame
- setup(): void — adds event listeners
- teardown(): void — removes event listeners

Call event.preventDefault() in the keydown handler for game keys to prevent the browser from scrolling when pressing arrow keys or space. The GameEngine should create an InputManager instance, call setup() on start, teardown() on stop, and call resetFrameState() at the end of each frame.
acceptanceCriteria:
- lib/game/input.ts exports an InputManager class
- Tracks keydown/keyup state for ArrowLeft, ArrowRight, ArrowUp, ArrowDown, a, w, s, d, space, Escape, Enter, p
- isKeyDown(key) returns true while a key is held down
- isKeyDown(key) returns false after the key is released
- wasKeyPressed(key) returns true only on the first frame a key transitions to down
- wasKeyPressed(key) returns false on subsequent frames even if key is still held
- resetFrameState() clears the just-pressed set
- event.preventDefault() is called for game keys to prevent page scrolling
- setup() adds event listeners on document
- teardown() removes event listeners cleanly
- GameEngine integrates InputManager into the game loop

title: Player 8-bit sprite renders on canvas using pixel color array
description:
Create a sprite system at lib/game/sprites.ts that defines 8-bit pixel art sprites as 2D arrays of color values. Each sprite is a number[][] where each number maps to a color in a palette array, and 0 means transparent.

Define a color palette as an array of hex color strings, e.g.: ['transparent', '#e8210a' (red), '#f0b088' (skin), '#0048e8' (blue), '#6b3e08' (brown), '#f8f8f8' (white)]. Define the player sprite as a 16x16 grid of palette indices forming a recognizable humanoid character: a red hat/cap on top, a skin-colored face with simple eyes, a blue shirt/overalls body, and brown shoes at the bottom. Keep the design simple but recognizable.

Create a renderSprite(ctx: CanvasRenderingContext2D, sprite: number[][], palette: string[], x: number, y: number, scale: number) function that iterates over the 2D array and draws a filled rectangle of size (scale x scale) for each non-zero cell, using the palette color.

Create a Player class in lib/game/player.ts with x, y, width, height properties. Width and height should be 16 (the sprite size). In the PLAYING state render logic of the GameEngine, create a Player instance at position (32, 200) and call renderSprite to draw the player sprite at (player.x, player.y) with scale=1.
acceptanceCriteria:
- lib/game/sprites.ts exports sprite data and a renderSprite function
- A color palette array maps indices to hex color strings
- Player sprite is defined as a 16x16 grid of palette indices
- The sprite depicts a humanoid character with hat, face, body, and shoes
- The sprite uses at least 4 distinct colors
- renderSprite draws each non-zero pixel as a filled rectangle
- renderSprite respects the scale parameter for pixel size
- Zero/transparent pixels are not drawn
- lib/game/player.ts exports a Player class with x, y, width, height
- Player sprite renders visibly on the canvas in PLAYING state
- The character is recognizable as a small humanoid figure

title: Player moves left and right with arrow keys and WASD
description:
Add horizontal movement to the Player class in lib/game/player.ts. Add a vx (velocity x) property initialized to 0, and a constant MOVE_SPEED (define in lib/game/constants.ts, value: 80 pixels per second).

In the Player's update(dt, input) method, check the InputManager: if ArrowLeft or 'a' is down, set vx = -MOVE_SPEED. If ArrowRight or 'd' is down, set vx = MOVE_SPEED. If neither is down, set vx = 0. Then update x: this.x += this.vx * dt.

Add a facing direction property ('left' or 'right') that updates based on movement direction. When rendering, if facing left, flip the sprite horizontally by iterating the sprite rows in reverse column order (or by using ctx.save/scale(-1,1)/translate and ctx.restore).

Create lib/game/constants.ts to hold all game constants. Start with PLAYER_MOVE_SPEED = 80. The GameEngine should call player.update(dt, inputManager) in the PLAYING state update, and render the player sprite in the PLAYING state render.
acceptanceCriteria:
- Player has a vx property and uses PLAYER_MOVE_SPEED from constants.ts
- Pressing ArrowLeft or 'a' moves the player left
- Pressing ArrowRight or 'd' moves the player right
- Releasing movement keys stops horizontal movement
- Player position updates smoothly based on deltaTime
- Player has a facing direction that changes with movement
- Sprite flips horizontally when facing left
- lib/game/constants.ts exists with PLAYER_MOVE_SPEED = 80
- Movement feels responsive with no input lag

title: Gravity pulls player down each frame
description:
Add vertical physics to the Player class. Add a vy (velocity y) property initialized to 0 and an onGround boolean initialized to false. Define GRAVITY = 600 and MAX_FALL_SPEED = 300 in constants.ts (pixels per second and pixels per second respectively).

In player.update(dt, input), apply gravity: this.vy += GRAVITY * dt. Cap vy at MAX_FALL_SPEED so the player does not accelerate infinitely. Then update y: this.y += this.vy * dt.

For now, add a temporary ground check: if player.y + player.height > 224 (near the bottom of the 240-pixel canvas), set player.y = 224 - player.height, player.vy = 0, and player.onGround = true. Otherwise set onGround = false. This temporary floor will be replaced by proper tile collision in a later feature.

The player should visibly fall when spawned above the ground level and stop when hitting the temporary floor.
acceptanceCriteria:
- Player has vy property starting at 0
- GRAVITY (600) and MAX_FALL_SPEED (300) are defined in constants.ts
- Gravity increases vy each frame: vy += GRAVITY * dt
- vy is capped at MAX_FALL_SPEED
- Player y position updates: y += vy * dt
- Temporary ground collision stops the player at y = 224 - height
- Player onGround is true when resting on the temporary floor
- Player onGround is false when in the air
- Player visibly falls and lands when the game starts

title: Player stands on ground and platform tiles via collision detection
description:
Create a physics/collision module at lib/game/physics.ts that implements AABB (axis-aligned bounding box) collision detection between the player and the tile map.

Create a function resolveCollisions(player, tileMap, tileSize) that checks if the player's bounding box overlaps any solid tiles. The approach:
1. Calculate which tiles the player overlaps using integer division of player bounds by tileSize
2. For each overlapping solid tile, calculate the overlap on each axis
3. Resolve by pushing the player out on the axis of minimum penetration
4. For downward collisions (player landing on top of a tile), set player.vy = 0 and player.onGround = true
5. For upward collisions (player hitting head on tile above), set player.vy = 0
6. For horizontal collisions, set player.vx = 0 and stop horizontal movement

Remove the temporary ground check from the player. Instead, call resolveCollisions after updating the player position each frame. The player should now stand on any solid tile in the level, not just the hardcoded y=224 floor. This requires the tile-based level (feature 14) to exist, so mark it as a dependency.
acceptanceCriteria:
- lib/game/physics.ts exports a resolveCollisions function
- AABB collision detection checks player against solid tiles in the tile map
- Player stops when landing on top of a solid tile (vy = 0, onGround = true)
- Player stops when hitting head on a tile above (vy = 0)
- Player stops when walking into a solid tile horizontally
- The temporary hardcoded ground check is removed
- Player correctly stands on platforms at various heights
- Player does not fall through single-tile-wide platforms
- Collision resolution handles corners without getting stuck

title: Player jumps with spacebar when grounded with no double-jump
description:
Add jumping to the Player class. Define JUMP_VELOCITY = 250 in constants.ts (pixels per second, upward).

In player.update(), check the InputManager: if space (or 'w' or ArrowUp) wasKeyPressed AND player.onGround is true, set player.vy = -JUMP_VELOCITY (negative because y-axis points down in canvas). Also set player.onGround = false immediately to prevent multi-frame jumps.

The jump should feel responsive: the player launches upward immediately on key press, arcs through the air due to gravity, and lands back on the ground. Double-jumping must NOT be possible — pressing space while airborne does nothing.

Optionally add variable jump height: if the player releases the jump key while still moving upward (vy < 0), cut vy in half. This gives a short hop on a tap and a full jump on a hold, which feels much better for platforming.
acceptanceCriteria:
- JUMP_VELOCITY = 250 is defined in constants.ts
- Pressing space/w/ArrowUp while onGround makes the player jump
- Jump sets vy = -JUMP_VELOCITY and onGround = false
- Player cannot jump while already in the air (no double jump)
- Player follows a natural arc: rises, slows, falls back down
- Player lands on the ground and can jump again
- Optional: releasing jump key early reduces vy for variable jump height
- Jump feels responsive with no delay between keypress and movement

title: Tile-based level renders from 2D number array with ground and platforms
description:
Create a level system at lib/game/level.ts and lib/game/data/level1.ts. Define the level as a 2D number array (15 rows by 64 columns for a horizontally scrolling level). Each number represents a tile type.

Define tile types in constants.ts: TILE_EMPTY = 0, TILE_GROUND = 1, TILE_BRICK = 2, TILE_QUESTION = 3, TILE_PIPE_TOP_LEFT = 4, TILE_PIPE_TOP_RIGHT = 5, TILE_PIPE_BODY_LEFT = 6, TILE_PIPE_BODY_RIGHT = 7, TILE_PLATFORM = 8.

Define TILE_SIZE = 16 in constants.ts. Create a tile palette mapping tile types to colors for rendering: ground = brown (#8B4513), brick = dark red (#A0522D), question = gold (#FFD700), pipe = green (#228B22), platform = gray (#808080). Empty tiles are not drawn.

Create a renderLevel(ctx, tileMap, tileSize, cameraX) function that iterates the 2D array and draws each non-empty tile as a filled rectangle at the correct position, offset by cameraX for scrolling. Only render tiles that are within the visible canvas area (256 pixels wide) for performance.

Design level1.ts as a fun Mario-style level: flat ground along the bottom 2 rows, some gaps to jump over, floating platforms at various heights, some brick and question blocks, and a pipe or two. The level should be 64 tiles wide (1024 pixels).
acceptanceCriteria:
- lib/game/level.ts exports renderLevel and tile type constants
- lib/game/data/level1.ts exports a 2D number array (15 rows x 64 columns)
- TILE_SIZE = 16 is defined in constants.ts
- Each tile type renders as a distinct colored rectangle
- Empty tiles (0) are not drawn
- renderLevel only draws tiles visible within the canvas viewport
- cameraX parameter offsets tile rendering for horizontal scrolling
- Level 1 has ground, gaps, floating platforms, brick blocks, question blocks, and pipes
- The level is visually recognizable as a side-scrolling platformer layout
